title: Chapter 50: The World Sees
content: Three days.
That's how long it took for the world to absorb what Wren and Ash had unleashed.

Wren watched it happen from the margins — from coffee shop windows and library computers and the borrowed phones of strangers who never noticed her standing beside them. She was a ghost observing her own story being told by people who didn't know she existed.

The broadcast had gone viral within hours. Every major news outlet led with some variation of the same headline: EXPERIMENTAL DRUG ERASES PEOPLE. The subject files were dissected, analyzed, shared, and reshared until they'd accumulated more views than anything NeuroScale had ever produced on purpose. Alexander Chen's name trended for two days straight. Petra Kovac's termination file was read aloud on national television by an anchor whose voice cracked on the word "terminated."

NeuroScale's stock crashed. Not a dip — a crater. Forty percent in the first trading session, another twenty the next morning. Investors fled. Partners dissolved contracts. The pharmaceutical giant that had shaped a decade of neural medicine was bleeding out in public.

Dr. Eleanor Vance was placed on administrative leave. The footage of her being escorted from Blackmoss played on loop — her jaw set, her stride deliberate, refusing to look at the cameras even as they documented her fall. She said nothing. Her lawyers said less. A spokesperson for NeuroScale issued a statement calling the broadcast "unverified data" and "an act of corporate terrorism."
Nobody believed them.

The ANX-00 launch was cancelled. Picked apart by regulators before it could reach a single pharmacy shelf. The drug that had made Wren invisible would never touch another bloodstream — at least, not through any legal channel.
These were the victories. Wren catalogued them carefully, the way a prisoner counts days.
But there were losses, too.

She and Ash had taken shelter in an abandoned transit station on the city's eastern edge — a place where the glitch-signals ran thick and tangled, easy to hide in. The walls were covered in peeling advertisements and the floor smelled of mildew and old coffee, but it was dry and it was dark and nobody came here.
From here, Wren watched the story grow.
And she watched herself disappear from it.
Because the world knew the drug existed. It knew what ANX-00 did — the erasures, the terminations, the clinical language of human disposal. It knew NeuroScale was guilty. It knew Vance was a monster.
But it didn't know Wren.

The subjects in the broadcast were data points. Case files. Numbers and names and status codes that scrolled past in the millions of renders and reposts. The public was outraged by the crime, horrified by the mechanism, galvanized by the revelation — but the victims remained abstractions. Alexander Chen was a trending topic, not a person. Petra Kovac was a headline, not a woman who had once loved gardens and spoken three languages and been erased at a desk by someone who never learned her favorite song.

Wren sat on the platform of the abandoned station and listened to the news play from a radio someone had left behind. The host was interviewing a bioethicist about the implications of ANX-00. The bioethicist used words like "troubling" and "unprecedented" and "a failure of oversight." He did not use words like Wren. Or Ash. Or any of the others who had survived.
They were ghosts in their own story.
Marcus published his investigation on the second day.

Wren had been watching him since the Underlayer — the young journalist with the too-sharp questions and the press badge he flashed like a weapon. He'd been digging into NeuroScale for months, and now the broadcast had given him everything he needed. His piece was long, detailed, devastating. He traced ANX-00 from its origins in a military-funded neural research lab through its quiet acquisition by NeuroScale to the clinical trials that had erased twenty-nine people. He named the executives who approved the trials. He named the doctors who administered the injections. He named Vance three dozen times, each instance a nail in a coffin she'd been building for years.
He was thorough. He was ambitious. He was everything the story needed.
He never found Wren.

She read his article on a library computer, scrolling past her own subject file without acknowledging it. SUBJECT 19 — WREN KOHL — ERASED. There it was, in black and white, on a screen anyone could access. Her name. Her status. Proof that she had existed once, and proof that someone had decided she shouldn't.

Marcus had searched for surviving subjects. He'd found records of two — a man in Seattle, a woman in Prague — but both had disappeared before he could reach them. He noted in his article that "the true number of survivors may be higher, but the invisibility effect described in the files makes verification nearly impossible."

Nearly impossible. Wren almost laughed. It was completely impossible. He could search forever and never find her, because the drug that had erased her had also made her unfindable. She was the evidence and the absence in the same breath.

Ash found her in the library later, standing between the shelves where nobody looked. He had a newspaper tucked under his arm — an actual print newspaper, as if ink on paper could make the story more real.
"They're talking about congressional hearings," he said.
"Good."
"They're talking about victim reparations."
"Good."
"They're not talking about us."

Wren took the newspaper from him. The front page was dominated by Vance's face, half in shadow, with the headline NEUROSCALE CHIEF DENIES KNOWLEDGE. Beside it, a sidebar listed the known subjects by number — not by name, by number, as if they were specimens in a collection.
She handed it back.
"No," she said. "They're not."

The painful irony settled around her like a second skin. The story was everywhere. It was on every screen, in every conversation, debated by every pundit with an opinion and a camera. The world knew what had been done. The world was outraged. The world demanded justice.
But the people at the center of it — the ones who had been erased, the ones who had survived, the ones who walked through a city of eight million without ever being seen — they remained ghosts.
The world could mourn Alexander Chen without ever knowing he collected vintage records. It could grieve Petra Kovac without learning her laugh.

And it could read Wren's file — SUBJECT 19, ERASED, a cold clinical notation on a screen — without ever understanding that she was standing right beside them, breathing the same air, carrying the same rain on her skin.
Ash leaned against the shelf beside her. The faint glow of his returning tattoos cast shadows on the spines of books nobody read anymore.
"So we won," he said flatly.
"We exposed them."
"That's not the same thing."

No. It wasn't. The victory was real — she could feel it in the data streams, in the slow collapse of NeuroScale's infrastructure, in every headline that named Vance as what she was. But the victory didn't include being seen. Not really. Not as people.
Wren pulled the Signal Key from her jacket and turned it over in her hands. It was warm, as always. Pulsing faintly, as if it was waiting for something.
She hadn't told Ash what it could do yet. What it might still do.
The story was out. The world was watching.
But the question remained: would the world ever see them?
